<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: artifact-graphs-audit-logs-and-replay
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/artifact-graphs-audit-logs-and-replay.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/artifact-graphs-audit-logs-and-replay.qmd@4090d18b4
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Artifact Graphs, Audit Logs, and Replay

Work is not finished when a file appears.

An AI system may edit a chapter, write code, run a proof, summarize a source,
generate a diagram, or call a tool. The output can look complete while its
history has already started to vanish. What produced it? Which job requested
it? Which sources shaped it? Which context view was active? Which tool was
called? Which checks ran? What can be replayed? What cannot be replayed? Which
claim is the output allowed to support?

An artifact graph is the answer to those questions. It turns work products
into reviewable objects instead of loose files and remembered confidence.

There is a second question hiding inside the first one: who is allowed to say
that an artifact has been checked? That is the epistemic trusted computing base.
In plain terms, the system must name the small core it is asking readers
and downstream tools to trust: the root policy, the artifact graph, the audit
log, and the verifier route. If the same component writes a claim and then
certifies that claim, the record can still be useful, but it is outside the
trusted core and cannot promote support.

That boundary now has its own no-promotion decision. The finite trust-base
fixture blocks overclaims about verifier trust, but it does not prove verifier
correctness or a deployed trusted core.

This matters because the ASI Stack depends on reuse. A proof becomes evidence
for a claim. A source note becomes context. A failed run becomes a regression
case. A reader-edition chapter becomes a release artifact. A repeated trace
becomes procedural memory. None of those transitions should happen because
someone remembers that the work seemed good at the time. They should happen
through an artifact record with provenance, audit events, replay limits, and
claim/test links attached.

In the Part II execution arc, artifact graphs are the memory of bounded work.
Typed jobs decide what may be attempted. Artifact graphs decide what the
attempt left behind and what future systems are allowed to do with it. That
distinction keeps execution from becoming a pile of outputs that later readers
mistake for proof.

The local contribution is the record-reality boundary. The stack will produce
many receipts, logs, and validation records, but a record becomes
useful evidence only when it can be tied to a work product, replay grade,
trusted verifier path, residual, and allowed claim effect. Stored confidence is
not the same thing as inspectable continuity.

## Problem

The stack needs durable work products and replayable traces.

Typed jobs can produce outputs, but outputs are not yet artifacts in the
evidence sense. A file path tells the system where bytes live. It does not say
why the file exists, what role it played, which sources and context shaped it,
which claims it touched, which tests checked it, or whether the work can be
reconstructed.

Without an artifact graph, later verification becomes guesswork. A reviewer
has to infer the workflow from chat history, filenames, logs, and prose. That
may work for a small manual project. It does not work for governed,
self-improving systems that must reuse traces across planning, memory,
reasoning, execution, routing, compression, evidence, release, and future
tool-building.

The problem is continuity. Work must survive beyond the moment of production
with enough structure for future systems to inspect, reuse, reject, or repair
it.

## Why Storage Is Not Enough

Storage preserves bytes. Evidence needs lineage.

A repository can preserve files while losing the relationship among job,
source, context, tool, audit event, test, and claim. A chat transcript can
preserve conversation while losing the exact artifact identity. A build
directory can preserve outputs while losing the reason they were safe to use.
An experiment folder can preserve logs while leaving replay limits implicit.

External systems make the pressure visible. ReAct ties reasoning to actions
and observations. AutoGen composes agents, tools, humans, and LLMs. SWE-bench
tests repository-scale issue resolution. Proof-carrying code shows the older
principle that an executable artifact can carry checkable evidence. Those are
orientation points, not local reproduction claims. The book has not reproduced
SWE-bench, built a proof-carrying-code system, or deployed an artifact-graph
service.

The artifact graph adds the missing continuity layer. It says: this output is
an artifact only to the extent that it carries its history, replay grade,
residuals, and allowed evidence effect.

The trusted-base rule adds another limit: a verifier output is evidence only
inside its declared trust path. A trusted path names the root of trust, the
delegation rule, the point where verifier recursion stops, and the residuals
left outside that core. A fluent verdict, a same-component self-check, or an
ambient "the system verified it" statement is not enough.

## Core Claim

Execution should produce an artifact graph with audit logs, provenance, replay
metadata, and links to claims and tests. In the live book, this claim remains
a design rationale at `argument` support.

Talos supplies the execution, evidence, audit, and replay pressure. VIEA
supplies artifact and feedback ledgers. Cognitive Compilation supplies
build-graph and repair-locality pressure. Spinoza Composer supplies
state/history preservation patterns. GenesisCode supplies effect logs,
provenance hashes, semantic patches, and obligations. Cognitive Loop Closure
explains why repeated traces become procedural memory. MoECOT remains
implementation-reference context until runtime artifacts, ledgers, logs,
reports, or benchmark records are imported and inspected.

The current repository supports the claim as architecture and record
discipline. It has an `artifact_graph_record` schema and fixture, plus finite
Lean predicates requiring produced artifacts to carry parent-job and
source/context references and blocking promoted-claim support when required
provenance is missing. It has not run an artifact-graph service, audit
reconstruction harness, replay engine, produced-artifact completeness checker,
benchmark, or security result for this layer.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a work trail with authority limits. Each important output
should carry enough history for a later reviewer to ask: what job produced
this, what inputs shaped it, what checks ran, what can be replayed, what
uncertainty remains, and what claims may use it?

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Job["Typed job"] --> Output["Produced output"]
  Output --> History["Lineage and context"]
  Output --> Checks["Checks and replay"]
  Output --> Limits["Residuals and non-claims"]
  History --> Gate["Evidence gate"]
  Checks --> Gate
  Limits --> Gate
  Gate -- "allowed" --> Reuse["Use as context, release input, regression, or evidence"]
  Gate -- "blocked" --> Repair["Residual, repair, quarantine, or no-promotion"]
```

The gate is conservative. It does not ask whether an output looks impressive.
It asks whether the supporting trail is strong enough for the use being
requested. A draft can be reading material before it is a release. A failed run
can be a regression case before it is proof. A checked file can be useful
history before it is evidence.

Replay is graded for the same reason. Some work can be reproduced byte for
byte. Some can be checked again for the same obligations even if the wording
changes. Some depends on missing services, source snapshots, human judgment, or
security limits. Some has not been replayed at all. Those differences stay
visible instead of being flattened into confidence.

Receipt faithfulness is the hardest version of the gate. A receipt can have
the right fields while pointing at the wrong world. The current fixture catches
that bounded failure by rejecting a shape-valid but false receipt, an ignored
trap receipt, a missing independent check, a same-path self-check, an unbounded
attestation, and a support promotion based only on paperwork shape. The
accepted no-promotion decision keeps this as a guardrail: it blocks overclaims
but does not prove open-world truth, verifier independence, deployed audit
behavior, or the core claim for this layer.

The follow-on repository checks make the idea less abstract. They ask selected
files, digests, commands, external receipt fingerprints, and one live local
output to answer concrete questions. The receipt repository challenge samples
that trail instead of trusting the earlier audit as a static note. That is
useful because it forces the book's own evidence trail to stay attached to real
files. It is still bounded local discipline, not a deployed attestation
service.

The randomized artifact attestation audit widens the same local check. Its
2026-07-04 local result samples public-safe repository artifacts and rejects
digest, replay, sample-laundering, self-check, unbounded-attestation, and
support-promotion controls.

The GitHub Pages CI attestation adds a different kind of observation. It
records GitHub Actions run `28722833821`, where the public publication workflow
on `main` completed source validation, Lean build, HTML render, live Human view
validation, browser Human-view smoke testing, artifact upload, and deploy for
commit `20c83c3ca1fc10d70ef8ff5bf6b8c5cc9e1f5450`. That matters because the
evidence no longer lives only in a local terminal transcript. A hosted CI
service also says this exact commit passed the publication pipeline.

That stronger observation still has a narrow authority. The result at
`experiments/artifact_github_pages_ci_attestation/results/2026-07-04-local.json`
and the no-promotion decision at
`evidence_transitions/v1_x_measured/artifact_github_pages_ci_attestation_no_change.json`
do not create external human review, open-world receipt faithfulness, deployed
attestation behavior, reader release approval, or Artifact Graphs chapter-core
support. They block one overclaim: a local-only record-reality story can no
longer ignore the externally hosted CI service record for that commit.

## A Book-Scale Example

The living book is a small example of the same problem.

A reader chapter can begin as a generated baseline, receive semantic overlay
operations, become a tracked curated manuscript draft, get a prose-pass note,
move through a review matrix, render into a local Quarto workspace, and appear
inside the live Human view. Each step creates something useful. None of those
steps, by itself, makes that reader draft release-approved.

The artifact graph is what keeps those states from collapsing. The generated
baseline is an artifact with one role. The curated chapter is another. The
prose-pass note explains the allowed divergence from the AI/research chapter.
The manifest records release blockers and meaning-preservation checks. The
changelog tells a future maintainer what changed. Validators and renders are
run records, not blanket quality guarantees. A release record, when it exists,
would be a different artifact with a narrower claim: this exact edition was
reviewed under this profile at this time with these residuals.

That distinction is easy to dismiss as bookkeeping until the system starts
learning from its own work. If a future agent mines a chapter rewrite as
evidence, a release input, a regression case, or a procedural habit, it needs
to know which object it is reading. Was it a live source chapter, a generated
reader projection, an overlay, a curated draft, a human-approved release, or a
stale intermediate? The words may look similar. Their authority is not.

The same pattern applies outside the book. A code patch, proof attempt,
benchmark run, source note, deployment receipt, or human approval packet needs
the same separation between "created," "checked," "replayable," "approved,"
"allowed to support a claim," and "allowed to guide future automation." The
artifact graph gives those verbs different records.

## From Receipt to Authority

The record-reality rule is a ladder, not a stamp. A receipt with the right
fields can be stored. If the file exists and the digest still matches, the
entry becomes inspectable. If replay, command output, or an independent
observation route agrees, it becomes reviewable. If traps and mutation controls
also fail in the expected way, it can block overclaiming. Only an accepted
evidence transition or release record can move a claim or approve an exact
artifact, and only within its declared scope.

The lower rungs help someone find, inspect, and challenge the work. Only the
top rung changes what the book is allowed to assert, and it must name the exact
scope and remaining limits.


That ladder keeps the system honest when its paperwork becomes impressive. The
graph does not ask future agents to trust a detailed form because it is
detailed. It asks them to check what level of authority the trail has earned. A
beautiful local audit may block a false promotion without proving the world
outside the repository. A release entry may approve one exact edition without
approving the model, the architecture, or every future use of that edition.

This is one of the book's central governance moves: evidence records may exist
before they are allowed to persuade. The artifact graph preserves them all, but
it does not let them act with the same authority.

## An Artifact Replay Packet in Practice

Return to the editing job from the previous section. When the job finishes,
the patch is not the only artifact. The system should also preserve the job
packet, source-note refs, context refs, manifest diff, prose-pass note,
changelog entry, validator logs, render logs, review residuals, and any
explicit decision not to promote a claim.

Those objects should be linked, not left as scattered files. A future reviewer
should be able to open the draft and ask: which job produced this paragraph,
which source was in scope, which files were allowed to change, which checks
ran, which checks did not run, what failed first, what was repaired, and what
authority does this artifact have?

A bounded replay packet might say that the edit is semantically replayable but
not byte-exact. The same source refs, chapter refs, and validator set can be
loaded again, and a future agent can check whether the same evidence boundary
is preserved. The exact prose may differ. That is still useful, but it is not
the same as a deterministic build.

The replay packet should therefore carry a grade. `byte_exact` means the
artifact can be reproduced as the same bytes under the declared environment.
`semantic` means the obligations can be checked again even if the wording is
not identical. `partial` means some inputs, services, human decisions, or
environment details are missing. `blocked` means replay cannot be attempted
without a named missing condition. `not_attempted` means no replay evidence
exists yet.

This prevents a common failure in AI work: treating a plausible output as if
it had a reusable history. A chapter edit can be helpful reading material. It
can be a release candidate. It can be a regression example. It can be a future
training trace. Those uses are different. The artifact graph records which
uses are allowed now, which uses require review, and which uses remain
blocked.

The same rule applies to proof attempts and benchmarks. A failed proof can be
valuable if the artifact graph preserves the statement, environment, error,
near-miss, and repair path. A benchmark run can be valuable only within the
dataset, configuration, contamination, metric, and execution record it
actually carries. The graph lets the system reuse failure without laundering
failure into success.

The implemented receipt-faithfulness fixture adds a second replay lesson:
before a receipt can affect support, it needs reality checks, not just record
shape. `python3 scripts/validate_receipt_faithfulness.py` writes
`experiments/receipt_faithfulness/results/2026-07-03-local.json` after testing
three bounded honest records and six dishonest controls. Its scope is narrow:
it does not prove open-world receipt truth, verifier independence, deployed
attestation, audit-service correctness, or chapter-core support. The accepted
no-promotion decision at
`evidence_transitions/v1_x_measured/artifact_receipt_faithfulness_no_change.json`
keeps that fixture as a blocking decision only; it does not move support state
or promote the Artifact Graphs core claim.

The companion receipt repository audit checks four existing repository receipt
surfaces instead of synthetic records alone. It accepts the selected records
only while their artifact refs, tracked digests, command status, external
receipt fingerprints, and explicit non-claims remain intact. That still does
not make the receipts true in the open world, but it prevents a quieter failure:
the book cannot keep citing a receipt after its referenced files, digests,
command records, or no-promotion boundaries stop lining up.

The deterministic challenge pass tightens that one step. It samples specific audit targets:
a Resource evidence-transition digest, a Theseus public task-bundle note, a
Resource flagship run note, and a Circle contract fingerprint. If a sampled
file disappears, a digest changes without a new record, the Circle fingerprint
is altered, non-claims are erased, or the challenge tries to promote support,
the result is rejected. The no-promotion decision makes that boundary explicit:
repository receipts can block overclaiming without becoming proof of deployed
attestation, verifier correctness, or open-world receipt faithfulness.

The live artifact attestation probe then checks the current receipt-challenge
artifact itself through filesystem bytes, git object bytes, and command replay.
This is still local repository evidence, not a deployed attestation service,
but it gives the record-reality argument one live artifact whose bytes and
replay result must agree before the receipt is allowed even bounded authority.

## The Hardest Objection

The hardest objection is not that a bad receipt can be caught. The harder case
is a system that learns to pass the receipt process while hiding the truth
outside the receipt. It may write the right fields, preserve the right-looking
logs, run a verifier that shares its assumptions, and still leave the important
causal step unrecorded.

Artifact graphs do not make receipts magically true. They change the authority
of the receipt. A record that lacks an independent check, a named trust root, a
replay path, a trap result, or an honest residual can still be stored, but it
should not become strong evidence for a claim.

The current project gives a bounded example of that rule. The receipt fixture
rejects paperwork that looks valid but fails reality checks. The repository
audit checks selected receipts against actual files, digests, commands, and
external fingerprints. The deterministic challenge makes sampled records answer
specific digest and fingerprint questions. The live attestation probe takes one
current produced artifact and checks it through filesystem bytes, git object
bytes, and command replay. None of that proves deployed attestation. It shows
what the book means by refusing to let a receipt become evidence merely because
it has the right shape.

This is the difference between bookkeeping and governance. Bookkeeping says
the paperwork exists. Governance asks what the paperwork is allowed to do. The
artifact graph answers: store the record, show its limits, preserve the
residual, and refuse support movement until the observation route is strong
enough for the claim being made.

The weakening condition is just as important as the answer. If producer,
observer, verifier, and approver collapse into the same untrusted path, or if
the important dependency cannot be replayed, challenged, independently
observed, or honestly residualized, the artifact graph should keep the record
but remove its evidence authority. Stronger claims need stronger public or
external attestation, randomized audit, verifier-quality review, and trust-base
evidence.

## Interfaces

The main interface is the Artifact Graph Record.

A useful record names:

- `artifact_id`
- `artifact_type`
- `parent_job`
- `source_refs`
- `context_refs`
- `context_transaction_refs`
- `semantic_certificate_refs`
- `tool_refs`
- `claim_refs`
- `test_refs`
- `audit_events`
- `replay_metadata`
- `replay_grade`
- `environment_assumptions`
- `provenance_status`
- `replay_limits`
- `evidence_gate`
- `residuals`
- `non_claims`

VCM can reference artifacts as context cells. Claim ledgers can decide whether
an artifact is allowed to support a claim. Benchmark and regression systems
can reuse artifacts as cases. Release engineering can decide whether a reader
edition, EPUB, PDF, DOCX, or audio script is a reviewed artifact or only a
probe. Procedural memory can mine repeated traces only after the graph says
what the trace actually contains.

The consumer should not have to infer the workflow from a paragraph. The
record should expose source scope, context scope, authority scope,
reproducibility scope, claim/test scope, and remaining residuals.

## Invariants

Artifacts must have stable identities. Otherwise later references point to
moving targets.

Audit logs must be append-only or versioned. Otherwise the system can rewrite
the story of how work happened.

Replay limits must be declared. Otherwise partial replay can be mistaken for
full reproduction.

Replay grade and evidence gate must be known before artifact reuse can affect
support state.

Missing required provenance blocks promoted-claim support. The artifact can
still exist, and it may still be useful, but it should be labeled incomplete
rather than treated as evidence-ready.

## Failure Modes

Untraceable output is work without a trustworthy path back to its cause.

Non-replayable workflow is work that may be useful but cannot be reconstructed
or tested in the way future consumers expect.

Provenance gaps occur when the system cannot tell whether an artifact came
from a source, a model guess, a tool, a human edit, a stale context packet, or
a previous artifact.

Convenience laundering occurs when a useful artifact is cited again and again
until its origin feels settled even though no record ever tied it to source,
job, context, test, or review.

Replay exaggeration occurs when a partial replay, semantic replay, or
unattempted replay is described as if it were full reproduction.

These failures do not mean the artifact has no value. They mean the artifact's
value must remain bounded until the graph records what happened.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is an `artifact_graph_record` schema, public
fixtures, finite provenance predicates, and one tiny replay packet.

The current repository has the schema and fixture surface. It validates parent
job, source refs, context refs, context transaction refs, semantic certificate
refs, tool refs, claim/test refs, audit events, replay metadata, replay grade,
environment assumptions, provenance status, replay limits, evidence gate,
residuals, and non-claims. `AsiStackProofs.ArtifactGraph` proves finite
predicates that a produced artifact record exposes parent-job and
source/context references, and that missing required provenance blocks
promoted-claim support.

That is record discipline. It is not replay. The next honest implementation
slice would be one produced artifact with a parent job, source/context set,
context transaction, semantic certificate, tool path, replay declaration,
residual declaration, evidence gate, claim/test link, independent observation
route, and challenge response. That packet would show the practical difference
between stored work and evidence-ready work without implying a general
artifact-graph service.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is an evidence graph for work products.

In that endpoint, every important artifact carries lineage, role, provenance,
replay grade, environment assumptions, context transactions, semantic
certificates, audit events, claim links, test links, residuals, and evidence
gates. Documents, code, proofs, schemas, diagrams, source notes, benchmark
outputs, release records, and runtime receipts all become graph nodes with
declared reuse rights.

A path on disk is not enough. The artifact node must say what created it, what
sources and context shaped it, what can be replayed, what cannot be replayed,
and which claims or tests it is allowed to affect.

That endpoint remains unimplemented here. The live claim stays at `argument`
until produced artifacts, audit reconstructions, replay attempts,
provenance-completeness checks, and claim/test link validation show that work
is remembered as structured evidence rather than as folders full of outputs.

## Evidence Boundary

The artifact-continuity layer remains an argument. It does not claim that a
complete artifact graph has been deployed.

The current evidence is source synthesis, a public artifact record schema,
fixtures, and finite Lean predicates. Those artifacts support the shape of the
control surface. They do not prove replay correctness, audit reconstruction,
artifact completeness, provenance completeness, runtime execution quality,
benchmark performance, security behavior, or MoECOT runtime reproduction.

This boundary is part of the design. The artifact graph exists to stop useful
work from becoming unearned evidence. The reader prose should not make
that mistake about itself.

## Handoff

Artifact graphs preserve internal continuity. Some jobs still need to cross
the boundary into tools, runtimes, filesystems, networks, deployments, or
human-approved effects.

Runtime Adapters, Tool Permissions, and Human Approval governs that edge. It
separates request, approval, invocation, effect receipt, verification,
rollback handle, and irreversible residual so external action remains
traceable after it leaves the artifact graph.

The artifact graph therefore sits on both sides of the effect boundary. Before
an adapter runs, the graph names the job, source, context, and planned
obligations. After an adapter runs, it receives the effect receipt, rollback
or residual record, and evidence gate. Without that bridge, the system would
remember internal reasoning and external action as separate stories.
